coredata.dc:title: Interfacial reconstruction via electronegative sulfonated carbon dots in hybrid electrolyte for ultra-durable zinc battery
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Hao
Luo, Zheng
Deng, Wentao
Hu, Jiugang
Zou, Guoqiang
Hou, Hongshuai
Ji, Xiaobo
coredata.dc:description: Water-induced side reactions and the growth of zinc dendrites are generally considered as the key problems hindering the development of zinc batteries. Herein, robust electrode/electrolyte interface is successfully constructed via introducing carbon dots (CDs) additive into hybrid electrolyte to enhance the stability of Zn anode. The CDs with -SO3 − groups deliver strong binding energy toward Zn, which can reduce the nucleation barrier, thus favoring the homogeneous Zn deposition, as successfully evidenced by theoretical DFT calculation and intuitive in-situ optical microscope observation. Meanwhile, the zincophilic CDs can be preferentially adsorbed on the surface of Zn electrode, further diminishing water-induced adverse reaction by virtue of the electrostatic repulsion between SO4 2− and negative charge of CDs. Accordingly, benefiting from the synergetic effect, the hybrid electrolyte with CDs endows Zn||Zn symmetric cell a prolonged lifespan over 4000 h with steady voltage fluctuation at 1 mA cm−2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemistry
Zinc battery
Carbon dots
Zinc anode
Electrolyte

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The mechanism of DMF-CDs on protecting Zn anode. a) 3D snapshot of 20 %DMF electrolyte system obtained from MD simulation and b) partial enlarged snapshot of Zn2+ solvation structure. c) RDFs for Zn2+-O and the coordination number. d) Binding energy of zinc ion on different sites of -SO3 −, Zn (001), Zn (100) and Zn (101). e) Schematic of Zn deposition on Zn anode with and without DMF-CDs.

Characterization of as-prepared CDs. a) TEM image of functional CDs. b) FTIR spectra of CDs. c) The XPS full survey of the CDs and deconvoluted high-resolution XPS of d) C 1 s, e) O 1 s, and f) S 2p spectra of the S-CDs, respectively. g) XRD patterns of the CDs. h) Zeta potential of different solutions. i) Ionic conductivity of different electrolyte.

Impact of CDs additive on Zn anode. Cycling performance of symmetric cells with CDs and without CDs a) 1 mA cm−2 and b) 5 mA cm−2. c) Rate performance of Zn symmetric cells at various densities. d) EIS results of symmetric cells after 25 cycles at 2 mA cm−2. e) Nucleation overpotentials test in Zn symmetric cells at 1 mA cm−2. f) CV curves of Zn plating/stripping in Zn||Cu cells at 1 mV−1.

Optical microscopy studies and EPMA analysis on Zn plating behavior. In- situ optical microscope images of Zn platting behavior with a) 20 %DMF electrolyte and b) DMF-CDs electrolyte in symmetric cells at 10 mA cm−2. c, e) Optical microscope images and corresponding height distribution of d) 20 %DMF and f) DMF-CDs electrolyte after 25 cycles at 2 mA cm−2 with a capacity of 2 mAh cm−2. EPMA mapping images of Zn anode in g, h) 20 %DMF and i, j) DMF-CDs electrolyte after 25 cycles at 2 mA cm−2.

Influence of CDs additive on Zn reversibility and side reaction. The SEM images of Zn anode after different cycles in a) 20 %DMF and b) DMF-CDs electrolyte at the Zn||Zn symmetric cell at 1.0 mA cm−2 with a fixed capacity of 1.0 mA h cm−2. c) Linear polarization curves showing the corrosion rate of zinc anode using three-electrode system. d) XRD patterns after cycling tests at 1 mA cm−2. e) Hydrogen evolution polarization curves in 1 M Na2SO4/20 %DMF solution with or without CDs additive.

The function of the CDs additive on Zn||NVO full cells. a) CV plots and b) self-discharge behavior of full cells with or without CDs in 20 %DMF electrolyte. c) Cycling performance for Zn||NVO full cells at 2 A g−1. d) Rate capability at different current densities from 0.2 to 4 A g−1. e) Long-time cycling performance at 5 A g−1. EPMA mapping images of Zn anode in f, g) 20 %DMF and h, i) DMF-CDs electrolyte after 1000 cycles at 5 A g−1.

head.ce:title: Interfacial reconstruction via electronegative sulfonated carbon dots in hybrid electrolyte for ultra-durable zinc battery

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water-induced side reactions and the growth of zinc dendrites are generally considered as the key problems hindering the development of zinc batteries. Herein, robust electrode/electrolyte interface is successfully constructed via introducing carbon dots (CDs) additive into hybrid electrolyte to enhance the stability of Zn anode. The CDs with -SO3 − groups deliver strong binding energy toward Zn, which can reduce the nucleation barrier, thus favoring the homogeneous Zn deposition, as successfully evidenced by theoretical DFT calculation and intuitive in-situ optical microscope observation. Meanwhile, the zincophilic CDs can be preferentially adsorbed on the surface of Zn electrode, further diminishing water-induced adverse reaction by virtue of the electrostatic repulsion between SO4 2− and negative charge of CDs. Accordingly, benefiting from the synergetic effect, the hybrid electrolyte with CDs endows Zn||Zn symmetric cell a prolonged lifespan over 4000 h with steady voltage fluctuation at 1 mA cm−2.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The increasing concern about the shortage of fossil energy and environmental pollution greatly stimulates the exploration of sustainable energy integration [1]. Although Li ion batteries have become the dominant energy storage technology for portable electronics and mobile electric vehicle [2,3], long-existing concerns regarding the potential security issues and insufficient lithium resources hinder the application of large-scale energy storage. Rechargeable aqueous zinc batteries (AZBs) have been considered as promising alternatives since intrinsic safety nature, low cost and uncomplex technology. In addition, advantaged merits such as high capacity (820 mAh g−1 and 5855 Ah cm−3), low equilibrium potential (−0.762 V vs SHE) and high ionic conductivity (greater than1 S m−1) make AZBs standing out among energy storage systems [4,5]. Nevertheless, the further prosperity of AZBs is still impeded by the poor cycling stability and fast capacity fading resulting from parasitic reactions and dendrite growth as well as dead zinc. The undesirable hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) derived from decomposition of active water in solvation layer of Zn2+ may influence the Zn deposition process leading to porous electrodepositions morphology, which also provokes the Zn corrosion to create the insulated by-products due to local accumulation of OH− [6,7]. Most disturbingly, uncontrolled dendrites growth stemming from inhomogeneous charge concentration and fewer nucleation sites would increase the contact exposure between Zn anode and electrolyte, which inevitably deteriorate parasitic reactions and generate inactive “dead Zn” [8,9]. Therefore, diminished water activity and uniform nucleation sites are of great significance to construct robust electrode/electrolyte interface.

Considering the direct contact between the electrolyte and cathode/anode, electrolyte optimizing among the recent technical explorations to facilitate reversible Zn batteries could effectively inhibit the dendrites growth and side reaction occurred in surface of electrodes. Accordingly, various tactics have been proposed to directly regulate the chemical environment of H2O in the electrolyte, such as functional additives, high concentrated electrolyte, hydrogel electrolyte, ionic electrolyte and co-solvent or hybrid electrolyte [10–12]. Additive strategies are widely used to enhance battery performance due to their simplicity, effectiveness and low cost, however, water-induced interfacial reaction and Zn corrosion could not be well inhibited. High concentrated electrolyte could greatly restrain the dendrite growth and superfluous reaction leading to high CE of nearly 100%, which is ascribed to the limited activity water, but the practical application is still impeded by increased viscosity, reduced ionic conductivity and expensive cost. Gel electrolytes have a wide application prospect in flexible devices owing to their high stability, certain flexibility and superior anti-freezing ability. Nevertheless, the worse rate performance and cycling performance when increasing the current density and areal capacity dramatically shadow development prospects. As for ionic liquid electrolyte, complex synthesis techniques and extremely high manufacturing costs make it confined to small-scale laboratory research. Co-solvent or hybrid electrolyte strategy have been widely accepted to tune solvated structure of Zn2+ cation, which could effectively reduce the activity of H2O molecule and suppress dendrite growth as well as maintain superior ionic conductivity.

Recently, methanol served as antisolvent was introduced into ZnSO4 electrolyte to coordinate with free water and Zn2+, which could minimize water activity and weaken Zn2+ solvation, thus a meaningfully greater average CE in harsh environments could be successfully realized [13]. Almost at the same time, ethylene glycol, polyethylene glycol and polyethylene glycol dimethyl ether are also applied to inhibit the dendrite growth and side reaction [14–16]. Besides the alcohols cosolvent, organic systems containing other polar functional groups such as cyano-group [17], ester [18], sulphone [19]and carbonyl [20] have been studied extensively to enable uniform Zn deposition and increase cycling performance. N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) featured with high dielectric constant (36.7 F m−1), large solvent dipole moment (3.86 D) and high solubility in water and organic solvent has been recently studied as a solvent for Zn batteries. Wang et al. reported a non-aqueous electrolyte of DMF solution containing zinc tri-fluoromethanesulfonate, which could facilitate dendrite-free Zn plating and alleviate dissolution of cathode material [21]. Ma et al. further introduce water as main solvent to avoid potential safety hazard and increase ionic conductivity. The DMF addition ensured a lifespan of 2000 h for symmetric cells at 0.5 mA cm−2 with a capacity of 0.5 mAh cm−2 [22]. Meanwhile, Li et al. adopt cheap ZnSO4 to replace expensive Zn(CF3SO3)2 and introduce 5 vol% DMF contents into aqueous electrolyte, however, the hybrid electrolyte just enable the symmetric cell with a cycle life of 420 h at 1 mA cm−2, which limits its further application [20]. However, there is still huge potential for performance improvements of Zn batteries.

Carbon dots (CDs), a novel zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial has gained high research enthusiasm in the fields of biomedicine, sensing, photoelectric catalysis and energy storage by virtue of good biocompatibility, abundant and tunable functional groups, chemical stability and unique fluorescence [23,24]. Herein, hybrid electrolyte based on DMF/H2O solution containing CDs additive was proposed to construct robust electrode/electrolyte interface. Notably, the introduction of DMF could regulate the Zn2+ solvation structure and reconstruct hydrogen bonds networks, which could effectively alleviate adverse effects of water involvement and improve voltage window. Most importantly, the functional CDs containing sulfonate anions (-SO3 −) with enhanced binding energy with Zn2+ ion could be preferentially adsorbed on the zinc anode surface and serve as nucleation sites to induce uniform Zn deposition. The electrochemical inert CDs could also reduce the reactivity of water and improve ionic conductivity due to stronger intermolecular interaction with water and DMF via restroction of hydrogen bond. Consequently, this successfully synergistic strategy can greatly extend the cycle life of Zn||Zn symmetric cells to 4000 h at 1 mA cm−2. Even at the high current density of 5 mA cm−2 and high deposition capacity of 5 mAh cm−2, the optimized electrolyte could still endows symmetric cell a long lifetime of 1000 h with stable voltage polarization.

Molecular dynamics simulation was employed to reveal the internal Zn2+ coordination environment and energies of HOMO and LUMO orbitals. As previously reported, Zn2+ is considered to be solvated with six water molecules (Zn[H2O]6 2+), and the solvated H2O molecules near the Zn anode are more likely to decompose than those in the bulk electrolyte leading to the formation of zinc hydroxyl sulfate (ZHS) and H2 [25,26]. As shown in Fig. 1 a and 1b, these H2O molecules could be substituted by DMF molecules after introducing DMF solvent. The radial distribution functions (RDFs) and corresponding coordination number (CN) distribution functions were further carried out to probe the solvation structure (Fig. 1c). The main peak of the Zn-O is identified at r\≈0.2 nm, which originates from DMF and H2O in the stable solvation sheath of Zn2+ in the 20 %DMF electrolyte. The average coordination numbers of Zn–H2O is 4.58, which suggests the substitution of H2O molecules with DMF molecules and reconstructed C = O··H-O hydrogen bond. Meanwhile, the comparison of molecular orbital level between Zn[H2O]6 2+ and Zn[(H2O)5DMF]2+ is exhibited in Figure S1. The position of the highest unoccupied molecular orbital (HOMO) in 20 %DMF electrolyte (-15.11 eV) is lower than that of H2O (-19.31 eV), implying that it is easier to lose electron when adsorbing on Zn surface, thus effectively suppressing the decomposition of H2O [27]. Subsequently, the density functional theory (DFT) calculation was carried out to study the interaction between Zn2+ and zincophilic group of CDs (-SO3 −). It is acknowledged that the weak adsorption between the Zn2+ and Zn substrate would give rise to continuously deposit on region of initial nucleation due to limited nucleation sites and lateral 2D diffusion, finally growing into dendrites [28,29]. As shown in Fig. 1d, the calculated adsorption energy of Zn ion with -SO3 − group on CDs (-6.23 eV) is much higher than the binding energy of Zn ion and Zn (101) plane (-5.24 eV), Zn (100) plane (-4.51 eV) and Zn (001) plane (-3.27 eV), suggesting the stronger Zn affinity of CDs, which is conductive to reducing the nucleation barrier and improve nucleation sites. Consequently, the possible mechanism of the collaborative strategy on constructing robust interface was elucidated in Fig. 1e. Within untreated electrolyte, the dendrites growth associated with interfacial adverse effect resulting from few nucleation sites and higher water reactivity would synergistically lead to poor cycling performance. When adopting the DMF as hybrid electrolyte and CDs as functional additive, the changed solvation structure of Zn ion, inhibited deprotonation of water, reduced nucleation barrier and constrained 2D diffusion jointly guide uniform Zn deposition to achieve an ultra-durable Zn battery.

Firstly, the 2 M ZnSO4 electrolyte with different volume ratio of DMF and H2O were prepared. Because DMF and water is miscible, all the electrolyte solutions show the superior solubility (Figure S2). However, the obvious white needle-like crystals could be observed in 30 %DMF electrolyte after standing for 2 days (Figure S3), which manifests that the stable solvation structure of Zn2+ with water is destroyed when excess organic solvent is added. Therefore, the 30 %DMF electrolyte may not be suitable for Zn batteries. Subsequently, the CDs with -SO3 − group were fabricated by typical pyrolysis method, in which poly (lithium 4-styrene sulfonate) and citric acid were chosen as sulfur and carbon source. The remarkable merits of simplicity, non-pollution and high-yield make it possible for potential application value in many fields. As can be intuitively seen from Figure S4, the brown and fluffy CDs powder could be well dissolved in H2O, 2 M and DMF-CDs solution due to abundant and hydrophilic oxygen-containing groups, which is a fundamental precondition for their application in aqueous electrolyte. Materials characterization of CDs is shown in Fig. 2 . Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image suggests that CDs with a diameter of less than 10 nm are uniformly distributed (Fig. 2a). In addition, the surface functional groups of CDs were identified by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. As illustrated in Fig. 2b, the absorbance peaks of 1172 and 1010 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of –SO3 − group. The absorption band at 1403 cm−1 was considered as stretching vibrations of the aromatic –C = C–. Two peaks at 771 and 678 cm−1 are related to = C–H out of plane deformation vibrations [30]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) test was further carried out to analyze detailed composition of the CDs (Fig. 2c-f). The full spectrum shows three typical peaks at S 2p (168 eV), C 1 s (285 eV) and O 1 s (532 eV), of which content is 5.95%, 69.28% and 24.77%. The high-resolution spectra of C 1 s can be attributed to four peaks, which are assigned to sp2 carbon atoms (284.8 eV), alcoholic species (C–O, 286.3 eV), carbonyl species (C = O, 288.1 eV), and C–S/C aromatic (289.3 eV). The spectrum of O 1 s exhibits four peaks at 532.1, 533.4, 534.2, and 534.0 eV, corresponding to O-C, C = O, O-H and O = S, respectively. In the high-resolution S 2p spectrum, two major peaks at 168.4 and 169.6 eV are characteristic of the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 states of the –SO3 −, whereas the two minor fitted peaks at 167.3 and 167.9 eV are associated with –SO2 − [31–33]. The XPS and FT-IR results further confirmed the presence of the sulfonate anions (–SO3 −) and other oxygen-containing groups, which indicates that the functional CDs severed as Lewis-base could interact with Zn2+ to reduce nucleation barrier thus guide uniform Zn deposition.

The result of X-ray diffraction (XRD) was also performed to characterize the crystal structure of CDs, as shown in Fig. 2g, a broad peak at about 20° could be easily detected, indicating an amorphous carbon [34].Zeta potential measurement was performed to evaluate surface charge state (Fig. 2h). The negative charge (-28.8 mV) of CDs in deionized water indicates the high affinity toward Zn owing to abundant functional group (-SO3 −, –COOH and –OH), which is in favor of repelling the approaching of SO4 2−, finally resulting in weakened side reaction. The more positive Zeta potential (from −14.9 to −4.2 mV) further suggests the adsorption of Zn2+ on CDs, which is conductive to inducing the homogenous Zn deposition [35]. Meanwhile, the ionic conductivity of different electrolytes could be also obtained from Zeta potential test (Fig. 2i). Not surprisingly, ionic conductivity gradually decreases to 15.5 mS cm−1 when the volume fraction of DMF increases to 30%, which is mainly attributed to the changed coordination environment in the presence of DMF. It is worth noting that the addition of CDs will slightly increase the ionic conductivity of the electrolyte due to the restraining of ion–solvent cluster formation [36,37], which is in favor of reducing the activity of water molecular and boosting the rate performance. Subsequently, as shown in Figure S5, the corrosion rate of Zn anode in different DMF content electrolytes could be obtained from Tafel curves. With the increase of DMF content, the rate of zinc corrosion gradually decreases due to the change of Zn solvated structure and reduction of reactive free water. Meanwhile, the gradually decreasing current density from linear sweep voltammetry tests at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 further confirm the effective inhibition of water decomposition. Although 30 %DMF electrolyte has the lowest water reactivity, the very low ionic conductivity and recrystallization of zinc sulfate may limit the electrochemical performance of Zn batteries. As shown in Figure S6, the contact angle is decreased from 81.8° to 76.3°, and 70.1° when the amount of DMF increases from 10% to 20%, and 30%, indicating a high electrolyte wettability and improved hydrophilicity in ZnSO4/DMF hybrid solutions. Notably, after introducing the CDs into 20 %DMF electrolyte, the contact angle is downgraded to 71.7°, further confirming the preferred absorption of CDs molecules on Zn surface, which is beneficial to induce uniform zinc-ion flux [10].

The electrochemical stability of the Zn anode in different electrolytes was evaluated by long-term galvanostatic charge/discharge test in Zn symmetric cells. As exhibited in Fig. 3 a, the pristine 2 M ZnSO4 electrolyte (denoted as 2 M) could only enable stable operation for about 90 h due to chaotic dendrite growth and consistent side reaction at a current density of 1 mA cm−2 with a capacity of 1 mA h cm−2. When introducing hydrophilic DMF into 2 M electrolyte in different volume proportions (the volume percentages of DMF were 10%, 20%, and 30%, and named as 10 %DMF, 20 %DMF and 30 %DMF, respectively), the electrochemical performances exhibit visible difference due to changed solvated structure and ionic conductivity. As illustrated in Fig. 3a, Figure S7 and S8, the 10 %DMF could steadily operate for more than 600 h at 1 mA cm−2, far exceeding the performance of 30 %DMF (150 h), which is ascribed to the fact that excessive DMF would reduce ionic conductivity and even limit reaction activity of Zn resulting in increasing polarization voltage and limited cycle life. However, insufficient DMF could not well inhibit the deprotonation of water and just enable Zn symmetric cell a lifetime of 60 h at the elevated current density of 5 mA cm−2. Notably, the voltage polarization of Zn electrode in 20 %DMF system suddenly drops at 900 h at a current density 1 mA cm−2, which means a short circuit due to consistent dendrites growth. Meanwhile, the Zn electrode with individual CDs additive works well within a test period of 1800 h with small polarization (Figure S9). In addition, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out to explore the performance distinction at different volume ratio in Zn symmetric cells (Figure S10). The 2 M electrolyte shows the lowest interfacial resistance (Table S1), which is mainly ascribed to the fact that the introducing of DMF would coordinate with Zn2+ and increase the viscosity of solution system. Meanwhile, EIS results of different concentration of CDs in 20 %DMF electrolyte indicate the 0.5 g L−1 CDs shows superior charge transfer capability, which is owing to the fact that the lower concentration of CDs could not provide enough nucleation sites and the excessive CDs may increase the viscosity of electrolyte system so that blocks the Zn2+ ions flux. Therefore, if not explicitly mentioned, the electrolyte with optional addition of DMF (20% volume ratio) and suitable CDs concentration of 0.5 g L−1 was used for further tests. Not surprisingly, the introduction of the CDs into 20 %DMF enables the symmetric cell steady operation for over 4000 h with a quite stable polarization voltage of about 50 mV, which is comparable to a four-fold increase in the lifespan of the Zn electrode in 20 %DMF electrolyte and 40-fold increase than that in pristine 2 M electrolyte. Meanwhile, when further improving the current density and deposition capacity to 5 mA cm−2 and 5 mAh cm−2, the Zn electrode could maintain 500 cycles with stable voltage hysteresis in DMF-CDs electrolyte, whereas the voltage starts to fluctuate at about 100 h and drastically rises, resulting in cell failure after cycling for 140 h in 20 %DMF electrolyte (Fig. 3b).

The rate performance of Zn symmetric cells at 1, 2, 4, 6, 10, and 2 mA cm−2 with a fixed capacity of 2 mAh cm−2 is displayed in Fig. 3c, the symmetric cell with CDs additive consistently exhibits a stable voltage polarization, even at a high current density of 10 mA cm−2. More impressively, when the current density switched from 10 to 2 mA cm−2 after 100 cycles, the symmetric cell still normally worked for about 400 h with a unfluctuating overpotential of 65 mV (Figure S11). By contrast, the 20 %DMF electrolyte could just run for about 80 h due to internal short circuit resulting from unfavorable dendrite growth. Most importantly, the DMF-CDs system exhibits lower voltage polarization, which is on account of the fact that CDs could preferentially adsorb on the Zn surface and serve as nucleation sites to induce uniform Zn deposition as well as improved ionic conductivity. The improved cycling stability and enhanced rate ability in Zn||Zn symmetric cells further forcefully confirm that the functional CDs additive with strong binding energy toward Zn could effectively suppress dendrite formation and alleviate adverse reaction. According to the evaluation parameters of Zn symmetric cells reported previously, including current density, areal capacity per cycle, cycle time. The performance comparison of plating/stripping reversibility of Zn electrode based on electrolyte engineering is exhibited in Table S2. Notably, the ultra-high cumulative plated capacity (5000 mAh cm−2) of the DMF-CDs modification strategy exceeds vast majority of previously reported methods, which makes it possible to overcome the issues of Zn anode and achieve large-scale commercialized application due to feasible, effective and cheap preparation technology.

The transport properties of the electrons and charges in cycled symmetric cells were investigated with EIS. As illustrated in Fig. 3d, the cell in DMF-CDs electrolyte displays much lower charge transfer resistance (Rct) than that of the case without CDs, demonstrating that CDs additive could improve the Zn2+ conductivity, which is consistent with the result of Fig. 2i. No doubting that electrode/electrolyte interfacial resistance (Rs) without CDs additive after 25 cycles is much higher than that in DMF-CDs electrolyte due to the fact that CDs additive could reduce the formation of dead Zn and by-product. The decreased Rct value both before and after cycling indicates promoted charge transport capability, which could account for the improved rate performance. The Zn||Zn symmetric cell without CDs shows a large initial nucleation overpotential of 52.5 mV at 1 mA cm−2 (Fig. 3e), which is downgraded to 38.8 mV with the help of CDs. It can be attributed to the fact that CDs with -SO3 − group can serve as nucleation site of Zn2+ ions so that the energy barriers could be decreased significantly [38]. Meanwhile, the lower overpotential is still found at higher current density (Figure S12). Furthermore, the chronoamperometry (CA) test (Figure S13) was scrutinized to study deposition process and the growth of Zn2+ on zinc anode. With an overpotential of − 150 mV applied, the current in 20 %DMF electrolyte increased continuously within 400 s, indicating a long and unordered 2D diffusion, which is ascribed to the fact that the absorbed Zn2+ ions diffuse laterally to deposit at the most favorable energy position and finally grow into rampant dendrites [39]. In comparison to 20 %DMF electrolyte, benefiting from strong binding energy toward Zn of CDs and more nucleation sites, the initial 2D diffusion of Zn ions on Zn surface with CDs additive occurred within 50 s, followed by a stable and continuous 3D diffusion process with lower current, indicating the deposition of Zn ions are guided to deposit adjacent region where the initial adsorption occurred, instead of the further area with lower surface energy, which is conducive to inhibiting vertical Zn accumulation on the local tips [27]. The galvanostatic overcharge test at 0.5 mA cm−2 was further carried out to confirm the above-mentioned results. As shown in Figure S14, The Zn anode in DMF-CDs electrolyte can normally work for more than 80 h without obvious voltage fluctuation, while that of 20 %DMF system is merely 20 h with uneven surface before cell short-circuit, revealing the effective inhibition capability of dendrite formation due to reduced nucleation energy barrier and constrained 2D diffusion. Moreover, the higher reaction kinetics with aid of CDs additive could be also confirmed by higher intensity of redox peaks in the cyclic voltammetry (CV) test of Zn||Cu cells (Fig. 3f). In addition, almost overlapped shapes after subsequent cycles indicate superior electrochemical reversibility (Figure S15) [40,41]. The reversibility of Zn plating/stripping was also investigated by calculating the average coulombic efficiency (CE) in Zn||Cu asymmetric cells. As shown in Figure S16, compared with lower stripping capacity of 731.1 μAh cm−2 (corresponding to CE of 97.55%), the cells with DMF-CDs electrolyte presents a higher stripping capacity of 853.3 μAh cm−2 (corresponding to CE of 98.66%), indicating less irreversible capacity loss of the Zn plating/stripping process.

By virtue of unique fluorescent properties of CDs, the adsorption of CDs on Zn surface was further demonstrated by confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). Before detecting, the cycled Zn anode was washed repeatedly by water and ethanol to remove residual electrolyte on the surface. It was found that almost no fluorescent signal was detected on the zinc anode operated in 20 %DMF electrolyte at 1 mA cm−2 for 1 mAh cm−2 (Figure S17), while obvious yellow fluorescence are observed on the Zn deposition layer with CDs additive, which further indicates CDs could adsorb Zn2+ and co-deposit on the Zn surface thus governing zinc deposition behavior. In-situ optical observation was conducted to further reveal the morphology evolution of Zn plating behavior in different electrolyte system. Some small and uneven tips appear on Zn surface after 5 min plating stemming from fewer nucleation sites and persistent 2D diffusion (Fig. 4 a). Zn protrusions are visible with the gradual increase of plating time and ultimately propagate to bumped dendrites. In contrast, as shown in Fig. 4b, the interface between Zn anode and DMF-CDs electrolyte consistently maintains homogeneous and compact Zn deposition on its edge and surface, which intuitively manifests that CDs additive could generate more nucleation sites and restrain the dendrite growth. Furthermore, the optical microscopy and the corresponding 3D height distribution (Fig. 4c-f) clearly indicate the triumphant suppression of vertical Zn accumulation. The surface texture and element distribution of Zn anode were recorded by electron probe microanalysis (EPMA). As demonstrated in Figure S18–S20 and Fig. 4g-j, a more uniform Zn deposition and higher content of Zn element could be observed in DMF-CDs electrolyte after 25 cycles at 2 mA cm−2. Moreover, the little signal of average S element content and decreased O element ration (from 8.7 % to 4.3%) suggest the great suppression of by-products, which agrees well with the previous EIS results ((Fig. 3d).

The pristine surface of Zn foil was shown in Figure S21. Then the Zn deposition morphology on Cu foil with and without CDs addition in 20 %DMF electrolyte was first detected by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) characterization. As exhibited in Figure S22, the deposited Zn shows massive sheet-like zinc dendrites in uneven cavities without the CDs assistance in contrast to smooth and dendrites-free surface in DMF-CDs electrolyte at 2 mA cm−2. The dense and parallel Zn deposition could be achieved even at current density of up to 60 mA cm−2 (Figure S23). In addition, the surface texture of Zn anode was observed to prove the effective inhibition of dendrite growth in DMF-CDs electrolyte at the current density of 1 mA cm−2 after various cycle times. As illustrated in Fig. 5 a and 5b, the Zn surface becomes rough and a small cavity could be observed after 25 cycles in 20 %DMF hybrid electrolyte due to uneven Zn deposition, and ever-increasing dendrites may impale glass fiber separator after 50 cycles, which further results in the fact that a great deal of glass fibers adhere to Zn anode surface and partial crushed dead zinc accumulates. When the cycle number is increased to 100, the crazy dendrite growth further accelerates the formation of dead Zn and a mass of black non-conductive by-products of ZHS pile up on the surface of Zn anode due to improving contact area between electrolyte and electrode, which would block charge transfer and even pierce separator giving rise to inferior cycling stability and even failure of batteries. In contrast, within the CDs containing electrolyte, there was little dendritic Zn on the Zn deposition layer during cycling process, which suggests. that CDs can guide homogeneous Zn deposition and thus suppress the growth of dendrite due to increasement of nucleation sites. The smooth and dendrites-free texture on Zn surface in the case of DMF-CDs electrolyte could be still maintained after 50 cycles. Even after 200 h of cycling, deposited Zn plates are parallel to the substrates with dense and uniform Zn surface and no obvious dendrites and ZHS formation. Meanwhile, the cross-sectional images of cycled Zn anode at 2 mA cm−2 with a capacity of 2 mAh cm−2 are shown in Figure S24. The thickness of the sediment layer continues to increase with the gradual increment of cycle numbers due to the heterogenous Zn deposition in sole 20 %DMF electrolyte. On the contrary, there was little dendritic Zn and by-product on the Zn deposition layer during cycling process, and the thickness of the Zn deposition layer always keep almost consistent, which manifests triumphant suppression of maniacal dendrites and uncontrolled Zn corrosion in weakly acidic aqueous solution, which is consistent with the EPMA results of Fig. 4.

Except for morphological evolutions of the Zn surface, electrochemical tests of Zn anode have also been employed to reveal the positive influence of CDs and DMF on water-induced side reaction. The Zn corrosion behavior was tested by Tafel polarization curves in three-electrode system (Fig. 5c). Obviously, the DMF-CDs electrolyte shows much lower corrosion current of 0.535 mA cm−2, whereas the Zn anode in 20 %DMF electrolyte exhibits higher corrosion rate (0.956 mA cm−2), suggesting stronger anti-corrosion ability [42–44]. When tested in Zn||Zn symmetric cells, the decreased corrosion rate could be also obtained (Figure S25). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of Zn electrodes cycled at 1 mA cm−2 after 20 cycles were further employed to identify the composition change of electrodes. As exhibited in Fig. 5d, a new small diffraction peaks corresponding to the (002) plane of Zn4SO4(OH)6·4H2O by product (PDF#44–0673) could be distinguished at 8.5° in 20 %DMF electrolyte, which could be ascribed to the fact that the continuous dendrite growth and formation of dead Zn would increase the contact area although the ZHS by-product could be inhibited in 20 %DMF electrolyte. In sharp comparison, almost no diffraction peak of ZHS impurity is detected on the Zn electrode after introducing CDs additive into 20 %DMF electrolyte due to the electrostatic repulsion between SO4 2− and negative charge of CDs. Not surprisingly, the successful suppression of ZHS by-products could be also achieved even after 100 cycles in DMF-CDS electrolyte (Figure S26). Notably, the stronger intensity of the Zn (002) plane for Zn could be observed due to the fact that CDs with -SO3 − functional group could preferentially guide the horizontal growth of Zn deposition along with the Zn (002) plane [45–47]. Digital photos of cycled symmetric cell (Figure S27) vividly illustrate the successful suppression of adverse reactions. Liner sweep voltammetry (LSV) was carried out to further unveil the suppression of HER upon interfacial adsorption of functional CDs. To avert the interference of Zn deposition reaction, 1 M Na2SO4 was used to replace 2 M ZnSO4 electrolyte. As exhibited in Fig. 5e, DMF-CDs shows a much slower increasing speed of cathodic current density than that without CDs additive and presents a larger overpotential, demonstrating that the CDs could reduce HER activity by virtue of reconstruction of hydrogen bonds between various oxygen-containing groups. Meanwhile, LSV scans from 1.5 to 2.5 V was also tested (Figure S28). Undoubtedly, the addition of CDs could suppress the decomposition of water leading to decreased current density, which may broaden the voltage window of electrolyte. Therefore,

To further illustrate the feasibility of the DMF-CDs hybrid electrolyte for practical applications, the full cells paired with NVO were assembled. The Na-doped V-based material was prepared via a modified hydrothermal method [48]. The SEM images of obtained NVO cathode material and corresponding XRD pattern are shown in Figure S29. As showed in Fig. 6 a, the CV curves of the Zn||NVO cells with or without CDs exhibit similar distinct and typical redox peaks, corresponding to the reversible redox reactions of Zn2+ ion, which indicates CDs additive does not participate in the electrochemical reaction. Note that a higher peak intensity and more coincident shapes could be observed in DMF-CDs electrolyte, implying higher reversible electrochemical process and higher electrochemical reactivity. The full cells with CDs additive show lower charge transfer resistance before cycling and after 1000 cycles (Figure S30 and Table S3), also suggesting improved the ionic conductivity for fast redox reactions, which is in consistent with the conclusion in Zn symmetric cells. The CE test was carried out to evaluate the effect of suppressing water-induced reaction by monitoring the voltage attenuation of fully charged Zn||NVO cells. As illustrated in Fig. 6b and Figure S31, more than 94% of the initial capacity is kept in DMF-CDs electrolyte after rest for 48 h, surpassing the value of 89.26% observed in the 20 %DMF electrolyte. Concomitantly, the Zn||NVO cell exhibits a superior ability of anti-voltage drop (0.30 V) than that of 20 %DMF electrolyte (0.44 V), which suggests CDs could effectively suppress the interfacial side reactions via strong interaction between Zn2+ and -SO3 − of CDs. Considering V-based cathode is gradually dissolved in aqueous electrolyte, discharge/charge process at low current density of 0.5 A g−1 was first performed to estimate the cycling stability. As shown in Figure S32, the capacity of Zn||NVO full cell in 2 M electrolyte exhibits apparent fade attenuation along with invisible inconspicuous discharge platform, which delivers a capacity of 166 mAh g−1 with capacity retention of 47% after 200 cycles due to structure collapse and dendrites growth. After introducing the DMF co-solvent, the improved capacity retention (63.4%) could be ascribed to reduced solvated water molecular. Compared with 20 %DMF electrolyte, the addition of CDs shows much better cycling stability (76.2%). Additionally, the long-term cycling performance of Zn||NVO full cells in different electrolyte and corresponding voltage-capacity curves are shown in Fig. 6c and Figure S33. The specific capacity only maintains 102.2 mAh g−1 with inferior capacity retention of 38.7% after 1000 cycles at 2 A g−1 in pristine 2 M electrolyte. The cell in 20 %DMF electrolyte delivers an initial capacity of 243.3 mAh g−1 and gradually decays to 147.2 mAh g−1. On the contrary, the Zn||NVO cells in DMF-CDs electrolyte shows excellent cycling stability, which still keeps a capacity of 187.8 mAh g−1 with outstanding capacity retention of 77.1%.

The rate performance of full cells in the electrolytes with and without CDs at various current densities from 0.2 A g−1 to 4 A g−1 was also tested to confirm the fast reaction kinetics and improved cycling stability (Fig. 6d and Figure S34). The full cell with DMF-CDs additive delivers superior specific capacity under all test current densities and 127.7 mAh g−1 is available even at a high current density of 4 A g−1. The capacity is recovered to 274.5 mAh g−1 when the current density switches back to 0.5 A g−1. In comparison, the cell without CDs additive exhibits a decreasing trend every eight cycles, suggesting inferior stability of the Zn anode. When further increasing the current density to 5 A g−1 (Fig. 6e), an initial capacity of 193 mAh g−1 can be observed in DMF-CDs electrolyte system, which is higher than that of 20 %DMF electrolyte (188 mAh g−1) due to the enhanced reaction kinetics. The Zn||NVO cell without CDs additive exhibits fast capacity deterioration and it just keeps a capacity of 95.8 mAh g−1 after 4500 cycles, while the cell in DMF-CDs electrolyte could consistently operate for up to 5000 cycles with a capacity of 119.2 mAh g−1, which could be ascribed to the fact that adsorbed CDs on the electrode and dissolved CDs in electrolyte can validly promote homogeneous Zn2+ ions distribution and suppress the side reaction along with dendrite growth.

The surface morphology of Zn anode harvested from Zn||NVO full cells after the CV test is examined to further assess the validity of enhanced cycling stability. As exhibited in Figure S35, the uneven surface with a mass of platelet by-product could be detected in 20 %DMF electrolyte, whereas Zn surface with CDs additive remains flat and homogeneous mrphology. Furthermore, the SEM images of the Zn anode after the CE test are shown in Figure S36. As expected, dead Zn and ZHS by-product could be observed on the Zn anode surface as well as some glass fiber adhered to the zinc anode due to the continuous dendrites. Exactly the opposite, the Zn anode still keeps its uniform deposition with dendrite-free morphology. EPMA was further carried out to investigate surface element distribution of cycled Zn anode after 1000 cycles at 2 A g−1 (Fig. 6f-i, Figure S37 and S38). The rough Zn surface with a lower concentration of Zn element and higher concentration of V and O element in 20 %DMF electrolyte, indicating crazy dendrite growth and harmful interfacial reaction (HER, Zn corrosion and dissolution of NVO cathode) during long-term deposition/dissolution process. After introducing functional CDs, the average Zn distribution ratio increases from 42.1% to 61.0%, whereas the contents of O and V element are down to 65 % of that in 20 %DMF electrolyte. The enhanced cycling stability is attributed to the preferentially adsorption of CDs on the electrode surface, which could effectively guide uniform Zn deposition and alleviate the dissolution of cathode.

In summary, a cheap and efficient electrolyte optimization strategy based on hybrid electrolyte via CDs additive was successfully designed and demonstrated to regulate aqueous Zn electrochemical reversibility. Firstly, the zincophilic and soluble CDs with -SO3 − group delivers strong binding energy toward Zn leading to reduced nucleation barrier and increased nucleation sites, which is beneficial for guiding homogeneous Zn deposition and rendering a dendrite-free Zn anode, as successfully evidenced by theoretical DFT calculation and intuitive in-situ optical microscope observation. Meanwhile, the CDs preferentially adsorbed on the electrode is conductive to suppressing deprotonation of water and reducing water reaction activity by virtue of the electrostatic repulsion between SO4 2− and negative charge of CDs, resulting in the alleviated parasitic reaction and prolonged cycle life. Most importantly, the restrained formation of the ion–solvent cluster and improved ionic conductivity are aroused from the adsorption of nanoscale-sized CDs toward Zn2+ and the reconstruction of hydrogen bonds between various oxygen-containing groups, which is conducive to suppressing dendrite growth and accelerating reaction kinetics. Consequently, the synergetic modified strategy not only endows the Zn||Zn symmetric cell an ultra-durable lifetime of 4000 h with stable voltage polarization at 1 mA cm−2, but also enables the Zn||NVO full cell an excellent cycling performance with capacity of 119.2 mAh g−1 after 5000 cycles at 5 A g−1. Therefore, this work inaugurates a new and synergetic avenue to prompt the development of all kinds of Zn batteries.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52074359, 51904342, U21A20284), and Science and Technology Innovation Program of Hunan Province (2021RC3014, 2020RC4005, 2019RS1004).
